claims.claim_text: Google SRE defines toil as work tied to running a production service that tends to be manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, devoid of enduring value, and that scales linearly as a service grows.
claims.rationale: The Google Cloud blog reproduces the SRE Book definition and its key characteristics directly.

claims.claim_text: Google SRE aims to keep toil below 50% of each SRE's time so the remainder can go to engineering work.
claims.rationale: The same Google Cloud source states that Google SRE aims to keep toil below 50% and preserve the other 50% for engineering project work.

claims.claim_text: The module's specific prescription to add more people until automation catches up is broader than the sourced Google guidance.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Increase staffing**: More people until automation catches up
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Create temporary capacity**: Free time for the team to reduce operational load and automation debt
claims.rationale: Google sources support restoring sustainable capacity, but not this exact staffing prescription in this wording.

claims.claim_text: The module's specific prescription to hand the service back to developers if it is too toil-heavy is too strong as written.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Transfer service**: Hand back to developers if too toil-heavy
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Rebalance ownership**: Revisit service ownership if the current arrangement is unsustainably toil-heavy
claims.rationale: Google SRE material discusses overload and ownership tradeoffs, but this exact directive is not directly backed here.

claims.claim_text: The automation-worth-it table reproduces specific payoff thresholds from XKCD 1205, such as '50x/day, 5 min, up to 6 weeks.'
claims.span_hint: section: Automation Strategies / XKCD's classic chart helps decide
claims.rationale: These exact thresholds are citable from the original XKCD comic, but that domain is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Google targets under 2 minutes of toil per on-call incident.
claims.anchor_text: **Google targets under 2 minutes of toil per on-call incident**. If remediation takes longer, it's a sign automation is needed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **If incident remediation repeatedly requires manual steps**, that's a sign more automation is likely needed.
claims.rationale: No allowlisted primary source was found for this exact numeric target.

claims.claim_text: Google SREs deliberately chose the term 'toil' because it implies unpleasant, grinding work rather than all operational work.
claims.anchor_text: **The term "toil" was deliberately chosen** by Google SREs because it implies unpleasant, grinding work — not all operational work, just the tedious kind.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **In Google SRE writing, "toil" refers to repetitive operational work**, not all operational work.
claims.rationale: Available sources support the definition of toil, but not this etymology claim.

claims.claim_text: Some teams measure 'toil velocity' as the rate at which toil is increasing.
claims.anchor_text: **Some teams measure "toil velocity"** — how fast toil is increasing. A positive toil velocity is a warning sign that the system is outgrowing its automation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Teams often track toil trends over time**. An upward trend is a warning sign that current automation is not keeping up.
claims.rationale: Allowlisted sources discuss tracking toil and trends, but not this named metric.

claims.claim_text: XKCD 1205 became a cult classic among SREs and is printed on countless office walls.
claims.anchor_text: **XKCD's "Is It Worth the Time?" chart** (xkcd.com/1205) became a cult classic among SREs for calculating automation ROI. The comic shows exactly how much time you can spend automating a task based on how often you do it and how much time it saves—printed on countless office walls.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **XKCD's "Is It Worth the Time?" chart** offers a back-of-the-envelope way to think about automation ROI.
claims.rationale: The popularity and 'office walls' claims are unsourced social assertions.

claims.claim_text: The war story names a six-person SRE team.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdotal specific with no source.

claims.claim_text: The war story says the team operated two critical services.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdotal specific with no source.

claims.claim_text: The war story says toil consumed 80% of the team's time.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Most of the team's time consumed by toil
claims.rationale: This is a fake-precise operational anecdote without attribution.

claims.claim_text: The war story table reports total toil of 118 out of 145 hours, or 81%.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story / The Toil Audit table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Total toil | Most team time | High |
claims.rationale: This is a precise unattributed anecdotal metric and cannot be honestly cited from an allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: The war story claims deployment automation saved 35 hours per week.
claims.rationale: This is a fake-precise anecdotal savings figure without attribution.

claims.claim_text: The war story claims auto-remediation saved 25 hours per week.
claims.rationale: This is a fake-precise anecdotal savings figure without attribution.

claims.claim_text: The war story claims self-service changes saved 30 hours per week.
claims.rationale: This is a fake-precise anecdotal savings figure without attribution.

claims.claim_text: The closing line gives an exact Google SRE proverb: 'If a human is required to take action because of an alert, that's usually a bug.'
claims.anchor_text: *"If a human is required to take action because of an alert, that's usually a bug."* — Google SRE Proverb
claims.suggested_rewrite: *"Alerts should be actionable, and routine manual intervention is a sign the system or alerting design needs improvement."*
claims.rationale: No allowlisted source was found for this exact quote in this wording.

further_reading.title: Identifying and Tracking Toil Using SRE Principles
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary Google Cloud guidance on defining, measuring, and reducing toil.

further_reading.title: Meeting Reliability Challenges with SRE Principles
further_reading.why_relevant: Connects toil reduction to broader SRE reliability practice and operational load management.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Self-Healing
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful background for the module's automation and self-healing examples in Kubernetes environments.

notes: No shared-pool source directly covers toil definitions, the 50% guideline, XKCD ROI thresholds, or the unattributed anecdotal specifics, so the pool was not used. Per task rules, claims inside quizzes, exercises, Mermaid diagrams, and fenced code blocks were not emitted; this means unsupported numeric specifics inside the fenced 'After 90 days' result block should be revised manually outside this seed.
